title: NA-Non ULIP
word_text:
Financial Freedom: What It Means and How to Achieve It?
Modern-day financial planning involves more than just earning a steady income or making the right investments. With the cost of living rising rapidly and responsibilities growing, even a small unexpected situation can make even a well-planned future tremble and fall apart. This often leaves most people feeling unsure about whether their financial decisions are truly leading them towards a stable future.
This is why financial freedom has become an important part of modern financial thinking. It brings structure to financial decisions and helps align them with real-life needs. Before exploring how it can be achieved, it is important to first understand what financial freedom actually means.
What Is Financial Freedom?
Financial freedom is a state where you have enough income to cover your living expenses without constantly worrying about bills or unexpected expenses. It is not about being wealthy or super-rich. It is about having enough financial security to support your lifestyle and make decisions without any stress. When you are financially secure, you can spend your time on what matters to you instead of focusing only on earning money.
Reaching this stage requires thoughtful financial planning. When your income, savings, and investments grow with a well-defined purpose, they support both your current needs as well as future goals. At this point, you can make financial decisions with confidence and clarity rather than pressure. Financial freedom, in simple terms, is the ability to live life on your terms without ongoing financial worry.
Key Differences Between Financial Freedom and Financial Independence
Financial freedom and financial independence are often used interchangeably or as synonyms for each other. Both aim to reduce financial stress, yet the intent behind each is quite different. Look at the table below to learn some of the key differences between financial independence and financial freedom
Main Steps to Achieve Financial Freedom
Financial freedom can't be achieved overnight.  It is a gradual process that involves building the right habits, making informed decisions, and staying consistent over time. Here are the key steps one needs to follow.
Live Below Your Means
Maintaining a lifestyle that costs less than what you earn is a key step toward financial freedom. While income typically rises with experience, increasing expenses at the same pace can limit financial progress. Allocating surplus income to savings or debt repayment helps build a stronger financial future. When expenses remain controlled, the resulting cash surplus can be used for future goals or financial emergencies.
Monitor Your Credit Score
A healthy credit score helps you secure better interest rates when taking a loan or refinancing existing ones. It may also influence the premiums you pay for car and life insurance. Regular monitoring and timely payments ensure your financial discipline leads to real benefits.
Create and Follow a Monthly Budget
Creating and following a monthly budget is a key financial habit. It helps prioritise essential expenses, maintain regular savings, and track progress toward goals. Over time, this routine strengthens financial discipline and minimises unplanned spending.
Set Clear Financial Goals
Everyone has different financial priorities, which makes it important to set clear and specific goals. Defining your exact goal and setting a deadline to achieve ithelps bring structure to your financial planning. Breaking goals into short- -term milestones makes them easier to track and achieve.
Eliminate Debt
To eliminate Debt strategically, you need to start by understanding when borrowing makes sense and when it does not. Debt can be useful if it supports long-term growth, such as investing in education, starting a business, or buying a home, where the potential returns are far better in comparison with the cost of borrowing.
However, using credit to fund a lavish lifestyle beyond your means can slow down your financial progress. High interest costs increase expenses and reduce your ability to save and invest. Reducing such debt helps lower financial strain and creates more room to build long-term wealth.
Develop an Investing Habit
Developing an investing habit starts with investing early to benefit from compounding. Increasing investments as income grows helps build wealth faster. Maintaining a balanced mix of equity and non-equity assets supports long-term growth. Review and rebalance your portfolio at least once a year to make sure that your investments are aligned with your long term goals.
Get Professional Financial Advice
Professional financial advice can help simplify financial planning. A qualified advisor helps create a financial plan tailored to your needs and goals. Selecting a certified financial planner ensures you receive trustworthy advice focused on long-term financial well-being.
Automate Savings and Investments
Automating savings and investments makes it easier to stay disciplined with money. Setting up automatic transfers for savings and investments helps maintain financial discipline without extra effort.
Prepare for Life’s Uncertainties
Life can bring unexpected expenses, which is why an emergency fund is essential. Having savings set aside for medical needs, repairs, or income gaps prevents financial stress. Keeping aside an amount sufficient to cover at least three to six months of expenses provides financial security during difficult times.
Track Your Net Worth
Your net worth is a useful measure of financial health. It compares your assets with your debts and highlights whether or not you are going in the right direction. Regular tracking helps identify areas for improvement and helps achieve long-term goals.
Maintain Good Health
The journey to financial freedom may have unexpected turns. Health and life insurance help protect you and your family from financial setbacks caused by illness, disability, or loss of income. This protection ensures stability even during challenging times.
Plan for Retirement Early
Retirement may feel like a distant milestone when you are still young and just starting your career, but starting early offers a strong advantage of compounding over the years. Saving even small amounts at a young age allows compounding to work over a longer period, helping your money grow steadily over time. Delaying retirement planning reduces this benefit and makes it more challenging to build a sufficient retirement fund later.
Do Regular Financial Checkups
Regular financial reviews help you understand how your finances are performing. Reviewing your financial plan on a regular basis allows you to assess progress and identify areas that need adjustment. Updating your plan to reflect changes in income, expenses, or market conditions ensures it remains relevant and effective over time.
What Is the 50/30/20 Budget Rule?
The 50/30/20 budget rule is an easy way to manage your after-tax income. It suggests spending up to 50% on essential needs like rent, groceries, utilities, and insurance. Another 30% can be used for wants such as dining out, travel, or entertainment, while the remaining 20 percent is set aside for savings and investment purposes.
This approach helps balance everyday expenses with saving for the future, without making budgeting feel restrictive. It allows you to enjoy your income while still staying financially disciplined.
Example: If you have a monthly in-hand salary of ₹50,000, around ₹25,000 out of it can be used to fulfil essential needs, ₹15,000 can be used for any kind of hobby or leisure time and ₹10,000 for savings or investments.
Ways to Know If You Have Achieved Financial Freedom?
You comfortably cover all living expenses without relying on your next paycheck.
You live within your means and still have surplus money left each month.
You are free from high-interest debt, such as credit card emi or high-interest personal loans.
You have an emergency fund that can cover several months of expenses.
Your investments are growing steadily and aligned with long-term goals.
You are on track to fund a comfortable retirement without financial anxiety.
You are consistently achieving the financial goals you set for yourself.
Money decisions are driven by choice and priorities, not stress or urgency.
You can handle unexpected expenses without having any impact on your future financial plans.
Money feels like a tool that supports your life, not a constant source of worry.
Key Takeaways
Financial freedom is about building a financially stable future and not about becoming rich.
Building disciplined habits such as budgeting, saving, investing, and debt management is essential to achieving long-term financial security.
Automating savings, tracking net worth, and monitoring credit health help maintain consistency and measure progress over time.
Preparing for uncertainties through emergency funds, health insurance, and life insurance protects your financial plan from disruptions.
Starting early with investing and retirement planning allows compounding to significantly strengthen future financial outcomes.
Regular financial checkups ensure your plan stays aligned with changing life needs, income levels, and market conditions.
Conclusion
Financial freedom is achieved through a balanced approach to financial planning, which includes saving, investing, and effective risk management. While building wealth is essential, protecting it is equally important. Insurance serves as a financial safety net, helping to maintain stability during unforeseen events. When combined with disciplined financial planning tips, insurance strengthens financial resilience and supports lasting financial freedom.
FAQs
What Are the Stages of Financial Freedom
The stages of financial freedom typically progress from gaining clarity about your finances, to becoming self-sufficient, building savings and stability, achieving flexibility, reaching financial independence, and finally attaining abundance where money is no longer a constraint on lifestyle or choices.
What Is an Example of Financial Freedom
An example of financial freedom is when someone plans early to build enough savings and investments to support a career change or early retirement without financial stress, like starting investments at a young age to create a sufficient retirement corpus by mid-life.
Can I Reach Financial Freedom While Still in Debt?
When you are still in debt, a part of your income would be directed to debt repayments. This might hamper financial freedom since you would have the burden of debt repayment. Thus, it is better to repay the debt to achieve financial freedom.
How Long Does It Take to Achieve Financial Freedom?
The time to achieve financial freedom depends on your goals and how soon you can fulfil them. If you have long-term goals and you need time to fulfil them, it might take time to achieve financial freedom. On the other hand, if you fulfil your goals soon and your financial obligations can be fulfilled in a few years, you can achieve financial freedom sooner.
Do I Need to Be Rich to Be Financially Free
Financial freedom does not require being wealthy. It is about managing money well and having enough financial control to support your needs and future goals.
Disclaimer
The content provided is for general informational purposes only. The material is compiled from publicly available sources, internal insights, and other information deemed reliable. While reasonable care has been taken in compiling the information, Bajaj Life Insurance Limited (Formerly known as Bajaj Allianz Life Insurance Company Limited) assumes no liability for its accuracy. Financial Freedom: What It Means and How to Achieve It?
Modern-day financial planning involves more than just earning a steady income or making the right investments. With the cost of living rising rapidly and responsibilities growing, even a small unexpected situation can make even a well-planned future tremble and fall apart. This often leaves most people feeling unsure about whether their financial decisions are truly leading them towards a stable future.
This is why financial freedom has become an important part of modern financial thinking. It brings structure to financial decisions and helps align them with real-life needs. Before exploring how it can be achieved, it is important to first understand what financial freedom actually means.
What Is Financial Freedom?
Financial freedom is a state where you have enough income to cover your living expenses without constantly worrying about bills or unexpected expenses.. It is about having enough financial security to support your lifestyle and making decisions without any stress. When you are financially secure, you can spend your time on what matters to you instead of focusing only on earning money.
Reaching this stage requires thoughtful financial planning. When your income, savings, and investments grow with a well-defined purpose, they support both your current financial needs as well as future goals. At this point, you can make financial decisions with confidence and clarity rather than pressure. Financial freedom, in simple terms, is the ability to live life on your terms without ongoing financial worry.
Key Differences Between Financial Freedom and Financial Independence
Financial freedom and financial independence are often used interchangeably or as synonyms for each other. Both aim to reduce financial stress, yet the intent behind each is quite different. Look at the table below to learn some of the key differences between financial independence and financial freedom
Aspect Financial freedom3 Financial independence 3
Meaning Financial independence means that you have sufficient income, which can cover all your living expenses while maintaining flexibility in life choices
Financial freedom is a state of life where you have enough assets and investments to support life permanently without having to work actively
Income source Includes both active and passive sources of income
Comprises mainly wealth and assets accumulated over the years, and passive income sources
Need to work Gives you freedom to choose your own working style, like full-time, part-time, or business
You no longer need to work and can focus on doing whatever you want, like following your long-lost passion
Lifestyle focus Allows you to choose work that matches your interests and values, rather than working purely for income.
Provides long-term self-sufficiency and stability so you can focus on enjoying your life
Time control Greater control over how you want to allocate time for work
Complete control over time, along with financial sufficiency
Planning approach Balances income, savings, and
investments and aims for wealth accumulation
Emphasises wealth preservation and legacy building
Long-term impact Supports a flexible and meaningful lifestyle
Enables legacy creation and long-term wealth preservation
Main Steps to Achieve Financial Freedom
Financial freedom can't be achieved overnight. It is a gradual process that involves building the right habits, making informed decisions, and staying consistent over time. Here are the key steps one needs to follow.
1. Live Within Your Means
Maintaining a lifestyle that costs less than what you earn is a key step toward financial freedom. While income typically rises with experience, increasing expenses at the same pace can limit financial progress. Allocating surplus income to savings or debt repayment helps build a stronger financial future. When expenses remain controlled, the resulting cash surplus can be used for future goals or financial emergencies.
2. Monitor Your Credit Score
A healthy credit score helps you secure better interest rates when taking a loan or refinancing existing ones. It may also influence the premiums you pay for car and life insurance. Regular monitoring and timely payments ensure your financial discipline leads to real benefits.
3. Create and Follow a Monthly Budget
Creating and following a monthly budget is a key financial habit. It helps prioritise essential expenses, maintain regular savings, and track progress toward goals. Over time, this routine strengthens financial discipline and minimises unplanned spending.
4. Set Clear Financial Goals
Everyone has different financial priorities, which makes it important to set clear and specific goals. Defining your exact goal and setting a deadline to achieve ithelps bring structure to your financial planning. Breaking goals into short- -term milestones makes them easier to track and achieve.
5. Reduce Debt Strategically
To reduce Debt strategically, you need to start by understanding when borrowing makes sense and when it does not. Debt can be useful if it supports long-term growth, such as investing in education, starting a business, or buying a home, where the potential returns are far better in comparison with the cost of borrowing. However, using credit to fund a lavish lifestyle beyond your means can slow down your financial progress. High interest costs increase expenses and reduce your ability to save and invest. Reducing such debt helps lower financial strain and creates more room to build long- term wealth.
6. Develop an Investing Habit
Developing an investing habit starts with investing early to benefit from compounding. Increasing investments as income grows helps build wealth faster. Review and rebalance your portfolio at least once a year to make sure that your investments are aligned with your long term goals.
7. Get Professional Financial Advice
Professional financial advice can help simplify financial planning. A qualified advisor helps create a financial plan tailored to your needs and goals. Selecting a certified financial planner ensures you receive trustworthy advice focused on long-term financial well-being.
8. Automate Savings and Investments
Automating savings and investments makes it easier to stay disciplined with money. Setting up automatic transfers for savings and investments helps maintain financial discipline without extra effort.
9. Prepare for Life’s Uncertainties
Life can bring unexpected expenses, which is why an emergency fund is essential. Having savings set aside for medical needs, repairs, or income gaps prevents financial stress. Keeping aside an amount sufficient to cover at least three to six months of expenses1 provides financial security during difficult times.
10. Assess Net Worth
Your net worth is a useful measure of financial health. It compares your assets with your debts and highlights whether or not you are going in the right direction. Regular tracking helps identify areas for improvement and helps achieve long-term goals.
11. Maintain Good Health
The journey to financial freedom may have unexpected turns. Health and life insurance help protect you and your family from financial setbacks caused by illness, disability, or loss of income. This protection ensures stability even during challenging times.
12. Plan for Retirement Early
Retirement may feel like a distant milestone when you are still young and just starting your career, but starting early offers a strong advantage of compounding over the years. Saving even small amounts at a young age allows compounding to work over a longer period, helping your money grow steadily over time. Delaying retirement planning reduces this benefit and makes it more challenging to build a sufficient retirement corpus later.
13. Do Regular Financial Checkups
Regular financial reviews help you understand how your finances are performing. Reviewing your financial plan on a regular basis allows you to assess progress and identify areas that need adjustment. Updating your plan to reflect changes in income, expenses, or market conditions ensures it remains relevant and effective over time.
What Is the 50/30/20 Budget Rule?
The 50/30/20 budget rule is an easy way to manage your after-tax income. It suggests spending up to 50% on essential needs like rent, groceries, utilities, and insurance. Another 30% can be used for wants such as dining out, travel, or entertainment, while the remaining 20 percent is set aside for savings and investment purposes2.
This approach helps balance everyday expenses with saving for the future, without making budgeting feel restrictive. It allows you to enjoy your income while still staying financially disciplined.
Example: If you have a monthly in-hand salary of ₹50,000, around ₹25,000 out of it can be used to fulfil essential needs, ₹15,000 can be used for any kind of hobby or leisure time and ₹10,000 for savings or investments.
Ways to Know If You Have Achieved Financial Freedom?
You comfortably cover all living expenses without relying on your next paycheck.
1. You live within your means and still have surplus money left each month. 2. You are free from high-interest debt, such as credit card emi or high-interest personal loans. 3. You have an emergency fund that can cover several months of expenses. 4. Your investments are growing steadily and aligned with long-term goals. 5. You are on track to fund a comfortable retirement without financial anxiety. 6. You are consistently achieving the financial goals you set for yourself. 7. Financial decisions are driven by choice and priorities, not stress or urgency. 8. You can handle unexpected expenses without having any impact on your future financial
plans. 9. Money feels like a tool that supports your life, not a constant source of worry.
Key Takeaways
1. Financial freedom is about building a financially stable future and not about becoming rich. 2. Building disciplined habits such as budgeting, saving, investing, and debt management is
essential to achieving long-term financial security. 3. Automating savings, tracking net worth, and monitoring credit health help maintain
consistency and measure progress over time. 4. Preparing for uncertainties through emergency funds, health insurance, and life insurance
protects your financial plan from disruptions. 5. Starting early with investing and retirement planning allows compounding to significantly
strengthen future financial outcomes. 6. Regular financial checkups ensure your plan stays aligned with changing life needs, income
levels, and market conditions.
Conclusion
Financial freedom is achieved through a balanced approach to financial planning, which includes saving, investing, and effective risk management. While building wealth is essential, protecting it is equally important. Insurance serves as a financial safety net, helping to maintain stability during unforeseen events. When combined with disciplined financial planning tips, insurance strengthens financial resilience and supports lasting financial freedom.
FAQs
1. What Are the Stages of Financial Freedom
The stages of financial freedom typically progress from gaining clarity about your finances, to becoming self-sufficient, building savings and stability, achieving flexibility, reaching
financial independence, and finally attaining abundance where money is no longer a constraint on lifestyle or choices.
2. What Is an Example of Financial Freedom
An example of financial freedom is when someone plans early to build enough savings and investments to support a career change or early retirement without financial stress, like starting investments at a young age to create a sufficient retirement corpus by mid-life.
3. Can I Reach Financial Freedom While Still in Debt?
When you are still in debt, a part of your income would be directed to debt repayments. This might hamper financial freedom since you would have the burden of debt repayment. Thus, it is better to repay the debt to achieve financial freedom.
4. How Long Does It Take to Achieve Financial Freedom?
The time to achieve financial freedom depends on your goals and how soon you can fulfil them. If you have long-term goals and you need time to fulfil them, it might take time to achieve financial freedom. On the other hand, if you fulfil your goals soon and your financial obligations can be fulfilled in a few years, you can achieve financial freedom sooner.
5. Do I Need to Be Rich to Be Financially Free
Financial freedom does not require being wealthy. It is about managing money well and having enough financial control to support your needs and future goals.
